With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I've decided to trace, as far as I can, my own occult lineages in photos. Juliet Ashley, whose photo featured in last week's Magic Monday, was a student of at least four significant occult teachers, and this is the first of them: Edgar Cayce, the famous "Sleeping Prophet," was born in 1877 and began having psychic experiences in childhood. He was a devout Christian all his life, reading the Bible from cover to cover every year and teaching Sunday school, but also saw auras, diagnosed illnesses in trance, and recounted his own and other people's past life memories dating back to ancient Atlantis and beyond. Juliet Ashley received several readings from him and spent several years studying his teachings. 

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Jupiter planetary charity
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Following your advice I've done the Jupiter planetary charity on following four Thursdays, hour of Jupiter. This is in connection with the divorce, that is now in progress. Should I keep doing the planetary charity? How long, how many sequences can I do? Should I recite the prayer / hymn as well? Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Jupiter planetary charity
replies.content: There's a lot of flexibility in the practice. You've made an appropriate offering to the planet; you can now wait for the planet to respond -- or if you like, you can make another offering, or take up the practice of planetary prayer. It really is up to you. If you've learned a method of divination, of course, casting a reading on the subject would be a good idea.

comments.title: Edgar cayce
comments.content:
Hey JMG

Concerning Edgar cayce, have you studied his teachings? And if so what is your evaluation of them?

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: It's been a while, but yes, I read some of Cayce's work back in the day. As you usually find with natural clairvoyants, his visionary material has a lot of symbolism mixed into it and some of what occultists call "astral static;" blind faith isn't a useful approach, but neither is blind rejection. There are insights worth studying in his work.

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: What insights did you find?

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: This was forty years ago! I honestly don't remember.

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: Is there a system that is well suited for a natural clairvoyant. Is Tarot enough?

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: I don't recommend tarot as a way of checking clairvoyance, because most people find it's very easy to interpret the cards the way you want to interpret them! Geomancy, horary astrology, or some other oracle that gives definite, unmistakable yes-and-no answers is a better choice in most cases.

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: I wonder bibliomancy where leans to, in this? towards the tarot or toward geomancy?

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: I've never had good results with it, so I'm not sure. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: Are there any general rules or guidelines to use when trying to distinguish the astral static from the wheat in this kind of visionary material?  Cayce has always interested me but I have heard about these difficulties in approaching his work.

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: First, always check clairvoyant insights against the evidence of the material plane, and gauge the accuracy of the visions in terms of what tends to be right or wrong in terms of facts on the ground. Second, keep track of what any given visionary is good and and what he or she is not so good at. Third, the Scots verdict of "not proven" -- neither a definite yes nor a definite no -- is always worth applying to such things.

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: Cayce made some interesting claims. Have you read Elisabeth Haich's Initiation? It also has some interesting past live and Egyptian claims. Entertaining nonetheless. I found that book because this band wrote a concept album about it. https://junius-official.bandcamp.com/

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce

replies.title: Re: Edgar cayce
replies.content: That's always one of my tests for the validity of past life claims. Anybody who claims to have been something really special in a previous life, and isn't somebody really special in this life, is just spinning fantasies.

comments.content: Dear JMG: is there any good writing on the effects of alcohol on magical / psychic work? I’m a very moderate drinker - not an ascetic, but the odd glass here and there. Middle way and all that. I was at a friend’s wedding recently, with much celebration and good cheer, and a bit more than I’d usually drink. I noticed the next day that I felt like my senses were just cut dead, totally centred on the mundane, really unable to capture the flow of my usual practice. It left me thinking about the massive psychic numbing that must be a result of the huge drinking culture where I live…

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This a passage in the Life of Apollonius of Tyana that discusses this. In ancient times, interpreters of dreams would refuse to refuse to listen to any dream which occurred during the first part of the night, during which the patient would still be under the influence of alcohol, precisely becuase it muddies the psychic waters and weakens contac twith the astral plane. It was for htat reason that Apollonius himself abstained from alcohol entirely, and I believe it is also why alcohol is included in traditional Christian fasting requirements along with meat-- both a vegan diet and abstinence from alcohol weaken your contact with the physical plane and open you to psychic influences. (I think it is also a more sensible and more balanced practice to then indulge and even over-indulge during the Feast which naturally follows-- after withdrawing to the spiritual world, one descends into the material and divinizes it. Joy at incarnation turns into wallowing in materiality, which provides the launching-pad for the next return to the Spirit, and so on. Modern life has no rhythms except for the occasional shopping season or month dedicated to venerating one of the clients of the Democratic Party, and so people are caught between perpetual feast and perpetual fast, unable to derive the benefits of either.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I haven't seen much about the subject, other than the two usual cautions found in the literature of occult schools -- either "don't touch the stuff at all," or "the occasional glass won't hurt but don't do practices when you're either under the influence or hung over." It makes sense to me, though, that alcohol would numb the psychic senses -- and yeah, a culture of heavy drinking would leave people pretty much blind on the inner planes.

comments.title: Healing

Hi JMG
As a Norse pagan I am trying to integrate a modified three caldrons visualization (A sort of 3 Chakras system) with Pranic Healing and Reiki healing practices.

Cealin

replies.title: Re: Healing
replies.content: I wish I could! You're doing important experimental work, and as far as I know (which may be wrong, of course -- I don't necessarily keep up with everything going on in the Heathen community) it's work that nobody else in the Heathen scene has done yet. The three cauldrons are very close to the three internal furnaces in Taoist practice, however, so you might look into East Asian sources for insights and inspiration.

replies.content:
I thought I read in one of your books about good results in healing using 3 cauldrons, but have not been able to find that comment again again.

Cealin

replies.title: Re: Healing
replies.content: I don't recall mentioning it -- and as far as I know, nobody in the Druid scene is working with this. Keep in mind that The Druid Magic Handbook, the book where I discuss the cauldrons, isn't standard practice -- it's one of my personal projects, aimed at getting an effective system of magic into the hands of Druids (and others who are interested).

replies.title: Re: Healing
replies.content:
Is it this?

"The ancient Taoist experts have acknowledged the significance of focusing on all 3 stages of our being, such as the physical body, the energy body, as well as the spirit. The 3 body systems match up with the 3 forces: The physical body matches with the Earth. The energy body of Man and Nature matches with the Yang force, often known as cosmic force (Yin/Yang combined). The spiritual body matches with the heavenly home. In the body of a human, we call these types of forces the Three Treasures (San Bao): Ching or Jing (life essence/sexual energy), Chi (the inside breath) and Shen (spirit or cognisance). They match up with the 3 Tan Tiens; the lower Tan Tien with the Earth/ Ching force, the middle Tan Tien with the cosmic /Human/Nature/ Chi force as well as the upper Tan Tien with the Heaven/Shen force."

The above looks like precisely the same set of energies/forces the DMH (and related) system works with. Did Taoist materials inspire your development of that system or was it more coincidental?

replies.title: Re: Healing

I was familiar with this via my work with taijiquan, but the cauldrons actually came from Irish lore. I forget the source, but one of the old Irish texts mentions three cauldrons inside the body that have certain magical powers. I borrowed that for the system.

Stephen A Mcnallen modified the practice from the old Irish source to include rune visualization then Wulf Ingessunu changed the runes and I incorporated JMG's dragon working and I put my own spin on it.

replies.title: Re: Blessings
replies.content: Once I finish posting the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose material, and catch my breath, I'll be posting at least some of the material from the Modern Order of Essenes, which is all about blessing. In the meantime, I recommend always getting consent, or if it's a situation where that's not an option, saying (aloud or silently) "I offer you a blessing" or some similar phrasing, acknowledging that the recipient can accept it or refuse it. comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I'm sure you've answered this one many times, but can you explain the difference between soul and spirit?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a tangled mess, because the words are used in very different ways by different people and schools -- and the way they're used in English squabbles endlessly with the way their equivalents are used in other European languages. In most European languages, soul (âme, seele) and spirit (esprit, geist) are fairly straightforward; soul is the emotional self, and spirit is the intellectual self. That distinction somehow didn't make it into English.

In Greek and Latin, by contrast, soul (anima, psyche) is the self and spirit (spiritus, pneuma) is the life force. That distinction somehow didn't make it into English either. As a result, no two people who use those words seem to use them in the same way!

In the specific traditions of occultism I work in, "spirit" means the core of the self that descended from the divine plane in the beginning of things and is gradually unfolding its potential in the form of various bodies -- material, etheric, astral, and (eventually) mental; "spirit" is often used as shorthand for the shells of spiritual, causal, and divine substance that we have but that haven't been formed into bodies yet. "Soul" is the accumulated knowledge and power the spirit has picked up during its incarnations, and is divided into three sub-aspects, called the conscious, intellectual, and emotional souls. Both spirit and soul remain from life to life, though the soul grows with each life assimilated.

But if you ask an occultist or religious person from a different tradition, you'll get a different answer!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One of the enduring realities of Celtic culture is that no two Celts can ever agree on much of anything for long. ;-)

I've done quite a bit with Ogham, and in fact have just cycled back into using it for my daily divination, after several years working with my Sacred Geometry Oracle. In my experience, it doesn't greatly matter which version you use, so long as you choose one and stick with it. The cards I use for Ogham readings are Colin and Liz Murray's Celtic Tree Oracle, partly because it's the only such deck that was available when I began learning Ogham as part of my work with the OBOD correspondence course, and partly because I love the pleasant, quiet, and understated artwork. When using that deck, I use the symbolism and order in the deck, even though I may disagree with it in other contexts -- and it's always seemed to work just fine.

So choose whichever option you prefer, and go with it. No doubt two millennia ago, Druids in different corners of Ireland taught the Ogham differently, and excoriated those who disagreed with them in true Celtic style...

comments.title: More possible evidence of a spiritual influence attuned to Jupiter

Last Magic Monday, two readers made comments regarding positive experiences in their lives that, as you commented after the second experience, seemed to you to suggest that some spiritual influence more or less attuned to the Jupiter vibration is making itself felt. I think I'm experiencing this same influence in my own life, and it may be wider than just my own life. Considering that it involves the mountain dulcimer, you may find it of interest for that connection alone.

This year, Linda Paulus self-published a biography of David Schnaufer, titled Pluck: The Extraordinary Life and Times of David Schnaufer. Because I was fortunate enough to take several workshops, including a week-long workshop, from him in the 1990s, I read the book last June. It's excellent; besides telling the story of his life - and the title is accurate - it tells enough of the history of the mountain dulcimer in the folk music revival that his life work is placed into context. He really is extraordinary, someone who found and followed his Awen and in the process brought inspiration to many people, myself included.

The first weekend of August, I attended the local festival, the Gateway Dulcimer Festival. Before the festival began, I used the book's list of his body of work to make a list of his music and his tab book to buy at the festival. While I didn't find any of it there, I did find one of the McSpadden David Schnaufer brand dulcimers on sale at the resale table. Until I read the book, I hadn't known such a thing existed. Before the first workshop began, I bought it and played it at all the workshops I took. It's beautifully made and really seems to contain his spirit. I'm practicing almost every day again, something I haven't done in years. Plus I started listening to the tapes I made of him teaching during the week-long workshop I took with him back in 1991 and learning how much he shared with us during that period to inspire us to become better musicians and people.

I didn't read the Magic Monday comments I referred to until Tuesday afternoon. A couple of hours later, the mail came. What should I find in the mail but the new edition of Dulcimer Players News, featuring a photo of him on the cover and an article on the biography plus other references to him within the issue! As I read the articles and noticed that 16 years after his death he remains a significant figure and inspiration for many in the dulcimer community, including for people who never met him, I got a funny feeling that Jupiter might be a significant factor in his life. The bio gives his place, date, and time of birth, so I pulled up his natal chart. Yes indeed - his Ascendant is Sagittarius, and it fits with his life story. Perhaps more evidence of the spiritual influence attuned to Jupiter having an effect? It's certainly having a positive influence in my own life at this time.

replies.title: Re: The 'Z' word
replies.content:
(Not OP. Also, feel free to redact bits of this comment if it turns out they touch on occult principles that shouldn't go in a public discussion; all I had to go on was armchair reasoning, there was nobody to warn me not to say anything.)

There's a grave problem here, at the intersection of justice, epistemology, and occult cosmology. When is it right to feel righteous about blaming someone for disbelieving in something, rather than feeling that, say, the universe itself stacked the deck against their having the necessary evidence, the situation is tragic, and it might be hardly their fault, if at all, but you have to bring the torches and pitchforks against them anyway?

This problem is part of a bigger problem that has been substantially wrecking my entire adult life, so I feel strongly about it. But just to focus on this part:

I have a situation I'm in, personally, of being caught between two contradictory moral universes, in which two contradictory sets of very strong expectations are being communicated by two contradictory forms of evidence. One moral universe is supernaturalist, and the other is in practical terms naturalist, even if my version of it is only effectively naturalist in a complicated indirect way. The evidences that prop up each moral universe are very surprising and troubling, according to any naive way of trying to guess what I might have expected in any straightforward version of the other moral universe, at least so long as I observe the rightful rules that prohibit cheating by letting my guesses have the benefit of hindsight.

In particular, the two moral universes make almost diametrically opposite demands upon me, in connection with the possibility of cryopreserving and eventually resurrecting the dead.

In the supernaturalist moral universe, all or almost all of the moral significance of natural things derives from the moral significance of the effects that those natural things will somehow ultimately have on supernatural things, directly or indirectly. People have information about supernatural things from sources such as revelations, miracles that seem to confirm whatever religion or belief system they happen in the context of, synchronicities, the private testimonies of their hearts, and so on. People can choose whether or not to attend to this information and act in the ways that the information seems to require. If they choose to attend to it, then they have a chance at being adequately good enough to not earn horrific consequences. If they choose to dismiss this information and act in ways that seem good to them according to purely natural cause-and-effect, then they risk earning horrific consequences. For example, a person could encounter information about miracles that appeared in the context of Buddhist religious practices, in a way that could be interpreted to lend credibility to Buddhism, the person could dismiss that information, and then they could thereby rightly earn eons of worldly suffering or even super-eons of hell-suffering. Or maybe a person could encounter information about miracles that appeared in the context of Christian religious practices, in a way that could be interpreted to lend credibility to Christianity, the person could dismiss that information, and then they could thereby even rightly earn literally infinite quantities of hellfire.

When I say that the supernaturalist moral universe's apparently-communicated expectations are "very strong", part of what I mean is that some of these supernatural belief frameworks, that the supernatural evidences are apparently lending themselves to interpretation as implying the truth of, are specifying punishments of enormous or even infinite magnitude. Another part of what I mean is that the expectations don't bend much, when one imagines a person trying to plead with them that they had evidences that might have seemed to instead indicate the reality of the other, incompatible moral universe. Christianity might say the person is ensnared by Satan and has moral hazard because of their wicked worldly attachments and desires and can't be sufficiently trusted to justly evaluate supposedly conflicting evidence, Buddhism might say the person is ensnared by Mara and has moral hazard because of their wicked worldly attachments and desires and can't be sufficiently trusted to justly evaluate supposedly conflicting evidence, and so on.

Meanwhile, the as-though-naturalist moral universe derives from the following observation about our world. For whatever reason, for us, literally no form of information of indicating supernatural influences on the natural universe is ever permitted to come into existence, if that information would create difficulties (by itself or aggregated with other information) for a sufficiently large proportion of the population to fit the information into a consilient naturalistic interpretation of the world, given that those people had sufficient inclination and the necessary knowledge. A naturalistic interpretation is one where everything has natural causes. "Natural causes" includes things like pattern-matching, hindsight bias, confirmation bias, file-drawer/selective reporting effects, wishful thinking/motivated reasoning, confabulation, temporal-lobe epilepsy, fraud, autosuggestion, social desirability bias, invisible cognitive triggers crafted by evolutionary selection for the strategy of occupying the "shaman" social niche, and selection of supernaturalist memeplexes for subtly manipulating people into supernatural belief in ways that nobody involved is even aware constitutes manipulation.

An example of this sort of consilient naturalistic interpretation is what happened when the rationalist Scott Alexander tried, in apparently good faith, to understand what kind of world this was, in which the findings from parapsychology could be what they were. He noticed that the effects failed to replicate at the strength one would expect if they were conventionally real and independent, only generating strong statistical signals from meta-analysis that could often be explained by errors in the assumptions of file-drawer-effect corrections, and that the purported supernatural phenomena seemed like they would have had to have too many other side effects on the world if they were real. He decided that the consilient world was one where the methods and safeguards of science weren't sufficient to protect against having scientific findings even in the case of the nonexistence of the phenomenon being studied, and wrote an article titled "Parapsychology: the Control Group for Science". He thereby unwittingly called the replication crisis in advance, and that's exactly the kind of correct prediction-by-generalization that one should expect if one had a correct consilient worldview. (I don't know how to communicate how obvious the part of Alexander's conclusion would seem, about the effects that supernatural phenomena should have had on the natural world, if one was to reason about supernatural phenomena the way a computer security professional would, but one was to only think about all the innumerable vulnerabilities that sufficiently motivated supernatural entities might use to break into the natural order using the claimed capabilities, and fail to think about the ways that supernatural entities might also be motivated to preserve the natural order. You just can't get a world that looks like this one from a hypothetical scenario that contains something functionally similar to demons but does not also contain something functionally similar to gods.)

To my knowledge, two other rationalists of Alexander's tier have also put in similar amounts of apparently-good-faith effort, on the order of a few weeks of spare time, and similarly concluded that

- there's definitely a process that causes people to frequently have apparent evidence that makes them confident of beliefs about natural things that later proved to be false, and that

- that process is already sufficient to explain people having apparent evidence that might seem to confidently justify beliefs about supernatural things without the supernatural things having to be true, and that

- there's side evidence that they'd also expect to see if the supernatural things were real, and they didn't see that evidence.

It just hasn't been as convenient for them to write about it.

There is also one rationalist-transhumanist of that tier who as far as I can tell used to practice GD and is currently either a believer or a principled agnostic, but I suspect that they would prefer not to be named.

When I say that the naturalist moral universe's apparently-communicated expectations are "very strong", part of what I mean is that the universe seems to have purposefully blocked off every route by which I might reliably prove to a critical third party that my own supernatural beliefs had not been caused in a "worthless", non-supernaturalism-requiring way. Because, of course, if I proved that my beliefs were caused by actual supernatural phenomena, then my beliefs themselves would be evidence that would compel the belief of the third party, and that's the sort of evidence that is forbidden from existing. And it's hard not to process this refusal by the universe as a communication of some kind of moral attitude.

I don't know what would cause the as-though-naturalist moral universe to appear real in this way. One hypothesis is that it's something to do with the "initiation of the nadir", where, during the current era, the world only produces value for the broader universe if people's practical beliefs at the population level stay mostly materialist. There's another hypothesis, which is one of the only hypotheses that comes close to concretely explaining everything I know. It's that the apparent supernatural universe is actually sort of a combination of dream, art project, education project, and/or creche, created by an advanced state of an intelligence or civilization that was itself rooted in a "real" universe that was natural or essentially-natural. The creator(s) would want to mostly only build up smaller intelligences that were capable of eventually growing up to help with the demands of the real universe, without the training wheels of a supernatural environment that they could take for granted.

In most of my hypotheses, the moral expectation that the as-though-naturalist moral universe is communicating is that people should maybe take the material world seriously, at least sort of as though it was the only real thing. In this context, it's hard to overstate how obvious the transhumanist project would be, to someone who really took that position seriously to the exclusion of the supernaturalist one. Death would be the tragedy from which most other tragedies ultimately derive, comparable only to stagnation and the failure to realize new possibilities for life. Every death would be the equivalent of a little burning of Alexandria. And there'd be reason to expect that a Moore's-law-like dynamic could be applicable to technologies such as massively parallel cryogenic vacuum atomic force microscopy forensic reconstruction of neural structures, so that after sufficiently many generations, restoring the frozen dead to a revivable state would mostly be economically realistic (assuming everything else had gone okay up to that point). And there'd be no reason to expect that the whole enterprise would actually be radically immoral because of unexpected consequences involving invading malignant subtle bodies.

(a discussion of e.g. Sean Carroll's diligent search for ways that particles with the necessary masses and interaction strengths could fit into applicable quantum physics, pertinent to decisions about whether to blame materialists for disbelieving in subtle bodies, would go here if I had time)

Maybe, if I thought hard about the different kinds of possible ways that the universe could be only trying to look naturalist, I could derive constraints on what kinds of cryopreservation/resuscitation procedures that those universes would be expressing a moral expectation to work towards or avoid working towards. I haven't gotten very far with that, though. (Like, funerals to detach the one growing intelligence, and anti-funerals to bring another compatible one? Maybe you would want to get phenomenology like walk-ins? Give up on resuscitation and just keep the dead for their memories, for the sake of posterity, so as to not burn billions of little Alexandrias?)

Meanwhile, in the supernaturalist moral universe, we instead get one more example of people choosing to dismiss their information and acting in ways that seem good to them according to purely natural cause-and-effect, and risking earning horrific consequences. People might encounter information about supernatural events that occured in the context of various occult practices and investigations, in a way that seems to imply some scenarios about how subtle bodies and reincarnation work over other scenarios, and then they could dismiss that information and conclude that there are few greater tragedies to be avoided than irreversible death, and then they could thereby rightly earn horrifying scenarios of societies populated by increasing fractions of bodies controlled by malefic spirits that have all the knowledge and habits need to perfectly imitate the former occupants, or whatever.

One of the aforementioned Alexander-tier rationalists is, so far as anyone can remember, literally the inventor of the term "steel-man", as in the opposite of "straw-man". This is someone who, as far as I can tell, has at least consciously gone far, *far* out of their way to try very hard to believe true things, in the face of contrary wishes and upbringing. They are also someone who others have commented as having a sort of "anti-reality-distortion field" or "reality enforcement field", by joking contrast to Steve Jobs's legendary interpersonal "reality distortion field", and they seem to have some deep unconscious psychological attachments to having the people and situations around them be normal. To have concluded anything else, they would have had to take into account the possibility that both the universe and their own unconscious influences were stacking the deck against their receiving the evidence necessary to truthfully conclude that supernatural things were real. It would cause me serious moral dissonance to say that that person is definitely culpable for failing to proactively give enough credence to that possibility.

It would be like if I was in Dawkins's thought experiment with the person claiming there's an invisible inaudible non-oxygen-consuming airborne-flour-permeable dragon in their garage, and then I decided to blame the skeptic for disbelieving in the dragon. Why not rather blame some other element of the overall situation? What if I instead blamed the dragon for inexplicably choosing to defy detection, or blamed whoever was coercing the dragon into defying detection, or blamed whoever had arranged the context such that if the skeptic was to disbelieve in the dragon then there would be morally negative consequences, or blamed the dragon-claimant for creating the conflict knowing that this would be the predictable outcome? Any of those would seem less potentially unjust than blaming the skeptic.

I mean, you can remove the idea of "blame" from the analysis of a situation like that, but then it seems to me that you would have a moral obligation to change the attitude with which you're bringing the torches and pitchforks. The target is just someone who's just trying very, very hard to live up to the illusory moral universe that the *real* moral universe might well have decided, with overwhelming force majeure, to seemingly deceive them into believing in.

replies.title: Re: The 'Z' word
replies.content:
Do you have a reference for those Soviet experiments? All I can find is articles about other Soviet experiments like head transplantation or mechanical reperfusion with oxygenated blood of killed animals who hadn't been frozen.

It's not easy to freeze and thaw neurons in such a way that they even biologically survive, even when it's a laboratory sample of just neural tissue, without any surrounding tissues complicating access. Typically it requires cryoprotectants to stop expanding ice crystals from crushing or rupturing or desiccating surrounding cellular structures, and other chemicals to help the neurons survive other physiological insults associated with the changes in relative biochemical reaction rates from colder temperatures. Skimming the literature, it was only about five years ago that people started reporting good survival rates for neurons in tissue samples in laboratory conditions. And then if you wanted the memories and skills to also survive, that would mean a sufficient fraction of not just the neurons themselves, but their axons, dendrites, and synapse macromolecular states, would have to make it through as well.

If an organism is frozen and thawed with a cryopreservation technology that isn't advanced enough to produce survival of a sufficient proportion of that organism's neural structures in-vivo, then damaged cognition and memory are already things you would expect to see, even if you were somehow in a world where subtle body loss turned out not to be a problem. (And survival of neural structures is something we can check for independently.) If that's true, then I don't see a way to avoid the conclusion that, if subtle body loss were a problem, it would require those technological improvements to happen before you could get the mere presence of damaged cognition and memory loss to be distinctively evidence that subtle body loss had been a problem. You'd have to have a situation where the specific degree or details of the behavioral damage were different from what you'd known to expect given just the cellular damage, in the direction that you might expect given a loss of subtle bodies. Maybe it would be suggestive evidence, but it wouldn't be distinctive.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Theologians have been bickering over the nature of the Holy Spirit since about fifteen minutes after the first Pentecost, and their proposals are all over the map. I have a suggestion, which I present tentatively -- after all, I'm not a Christian.

My take is that the Holy Spirit is a portion of the life force into which the Christian god is incarnate, in the same way that Christ is a portion of humanity -- a human individual -- in which the same god is incarnate. If this is the case, the Holy Spirit is the life force taken up into divinity and infused with the divine essence.That's why Jesus says (John 10:10), "I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly." The people he was talking to were already alive, of course, but the Holy Spirit in this theory is divine life, life full of the divine presence, and thus "more abundant" and then some.

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Some questions for you today.

1) Would a relationship with venus work to balance sun worship (esp the egoic tendency)?

2) I want to use horary to ask what martial art for long term self defense and health should i learn, but I don't know the signifier. Would you be able to guide?

3)You said something interesting about the level of consciousness one rises to in a lifetime after clearing karma. Does this imply that that one can have lifetimes where one's spiritual capacities are completely locked/one operates at a low level of consciousness?

Is there no sort of teleological function that brings you back to the level you exited the last lifetime at as a default?

4)
a) As an addendum to the last q, I have read things about how it in not just the actions in your life, but the momentum of your consciousness at your death that determines the nature of future lifetimes. Would you be able to comment/share more? Does this imply that a painful or slow death (say one where you lose your memory) reduces the momentum of the next lifetime?

b) How does once die well? There must be interesting readings on this you've come across. Would love some pointers.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) No, not at all. It would simply add vanity to egocentrism. You can balance the Sun with Saturn, or better still, with all the planets.

2) I'd use the ruler of the 5th house, which governs martial arts along with all other arts, crafts, and exercises.

3) No, there's no such function. If you have a lot of difficult karma to deal with you have to go back to the level at which you can deal with it. Once that's cleared away, mind you, you can advance very quickly.

4a) No, not at all. The momentum of consciousness at death determines how you experience the first stages of the after-death state, and that's all. Once you pass through the Second Death and start shedding astral energies, it's the overall nature of the life just finished that governs, subject of course to how much karma generated in this life you work off before you die.

4b) There are reams and reams of books on the subject. The very short version of the consensus is to start by realizing that you will certainly die, and it may happen at any point; regularly look back over your life honestly, accept your mistakes, and do your best to make them right if you can; practice recollection (remembering each day in reverse as you lie down to sleep) so that you take care of some of the processing of memories before you die; and cultivate an active spiritual life including contact with deity. There's much more you can do if you have advance warning of your death, but those will guarantee that even if it's sudden, you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content: b) Why is the recollection in reverse?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content: Because that's the way you'll remember it when you've died; the most recent memories go first, and things unfold from there. You might as well get in practice now!

comments.title: Relationships with divine beings
comments.content:
Happy magic Monday to you all! Thank you again, JMG, for this space; and thank you for choosing monday - I'm sure there are magical reasons, but mundanely it's lovely to have this to look forward to after the first day of my work week!

When dealing with spirits and the divine, I recognize that you often recommend divination and communication - particularly two-way with lots of listening to balance the speaking/praying/ritual. I'm feeling unsure as to how much to treat this interaction as a relationship. By this I mean, in reaching out to the divine, we are entering into an unbalanced power dynamic. I have been working with the Norse pantheon for a while. Recently I had a significant moment of interaction with a being that identified as from the Greek pantheon. In exploring the Greek pantheon, I've only become more committed to my Norse practice. Though Odin is harsh, I find more redeeming qualities in him than in Zeus, and in my study of Greek culture and religion, I'm actually identifying what feels to me as the roots of much of what I dislike about modern western culture. For all the animism and spiritual practice, it seems... dogmatic and sterile in many ways.

Direct questions:
Is it unwise to turn away a spiritual connection; and are there safer, wiser ways to disentangle from a divine connection?

Along those lines, I'm now reconsidering how I left the Christian churches of my upbringing. Are there ways to address and heal any divine offense I may have caused by believing (as I did then) that I could just mundanely leave years of worship and practice?

I often read and am recommended to pursue pagan practice by studying mythology and ancient cultures. I understand this can be instructive and guiding, but how much should it play into a modern relationship/spiritual-practice? I know you've recommended not imagining Gods in their media/art forms, but allowing them to reveal themselves. Is it appropriate to judge the Gods by their mythologies and the cultures that revered them? Should they be given another chance in modern times, or is it more a "if someone tells you who they are, believe them" situation.

Last, you mention above that systems like geomancy are better than tarot for checking oneself based on there giving of yes/no answers. Where do you see your sacred geometry oracle falling on that spectrum. I've found it surprisingly blunt in multiple readings, though there is room for storytelling and nuance. Do you see certain cards being as clearly positive or negative as the geomantic figures?

I appreciate your time!

replies.title: Re: Relationships with divine beings
replies.content:
1) You can always turn down a spiritual "friend request" so long as you yourself didn't invite it.

2) These are intricate questions, and great themes for meditation. There is no simple answer!

3) In my experience the SGO is midway between the nuanced but vague oracles such as the tarot and cut-and-dried oracles such as geomancy. The SGO can certainly say "sorry, you screwed up" and things like that with great clarity!

replies.title: Re: Relationships with divine beings
replies.content:
1. That's an interesting question. I need to examine my exploration and make sure being sensitive and receptive is properly grounded and does not bleed into active outreach.

3. That matches my experience, after much time with tarot, I was initially intimidated by the SGO's ability to tell it straight.

2. 'Fraid you'd write that... off to meditate ;)
